content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. Please recommend mindf*ck/mind-bender movies that are not it was all just a dream/imagination/simulation

**Score:** 47
**Body:** For so long, I've loved sci-fi/mystery/thriller movies that involve these themes.

I particulatly like time-travel/sciency movies and weird movies. 

I want to take a break from movies that are just imagination/dream/simulation and want to explore other movies that don't fall into these categories but are still amazing, though-provoking and has many plot twists.

I have watched a ton of movies already so I would tell you if I've seen your suggestions.

TIA!

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Pi

Parasite

Seconds

Twinless

Stalker

Primer

Sorry to Bother You

Open Your Eyes

I Saw the TV Glow

Everything Everywhere All at Once
  - Pi is a good one, I was going to list it but figured my list was already long enough.
2. coherence
3. Event Horizon 

Cube (1997)
4. 12 Monkeys
5. Enemy 

Primer

Brazil

Momento

Shutter Island

The Prestige 

Oldboy

Lucky Number Sleven

Edit to add one more: The One I Love
6. Run Lola Run
7. Naked Lunch
8. The Substance jacked me up pretty good. 

I see some love for Nolan, but thus far no mention of Interstellar. Hot Take: it’s better than Inception.
9. Triangle

## 2. Need a cool movie to watch with absolutely no romantic subplots or even a hint at one

**Score:** 33
**Body:** Chat I am kinda emotionally wrecked, I’ve been trying to watch more movies so I need another film to watch tonight to hit my quota and I was honestly gonna watch call me by your name for the first time but I think that’ll send me over lmfao 

You don’t have to the describe the movie I like going in blind I’ll just take the name and or year it released.

Comedies, action, or anything incredibly beautifully shot or thought provoking is a plus 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. “Nope” was the first thing that came to mind, for some reason. It’s an excellent film if you’ve never seen it.

Dredd (2012) is another favorite of mine and I don’t recall there being any romances.
2. 1) snatch
2) lock stock and 2 smoking barrels
3. Hot Fuzz, unless you count a bromance.
4. The Thing (1982)

Bubba Ho-Tep (2002)
5. Flow
6. Time Bandits.    

Wallace and Gromit: a grand day out and the wrong trousers 

Space Odyssey 2001

The Italian Job (1969).    

The Lady in the Van.    

Rambo.

Hot Fuzz.
7. Koyanisqaatsi 
8. School of Rock

Stand by Me
9. Dogma (1999)
10. you got me curious - why is there a quoata of movies you have to watch amigo?

one of my favorites: Prisoners

## 3. I am in the hills, it's chilly, it's dark, it's lonely. Suggest me a movie that goes with this setting.

**Score:** 28
**Body:** Hello everyone. Basically the title. Suggest me a movie that you think would suit this situation the best. I am on a holiday with my wife. 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The Shining
2. The Hateful Eight (2015)
3. Misery
4. The Ritual
5. Ravenous 1999
6. Braveheart
7. Black Christmas (74) 
8. It Comes At Night
9. Lawless (bonus points for Tom Hardy and Jessica Chastain)

Winter’s Bone (young Jennifer Lawrence is perfect, and Dale Dickey is terrifying)
10. Not a movie but Twin Peaks fits the vibe.

## 4. Movies about dirty cops (like series ‘We Own This City’, cops robbing drug dealers, etc)

**Score:** 27
**Body:** Would prefer more so from hears 2000-present, but not against oldies.

Only movies that come to mind are like 

Training Day.

L A Confidential

Anything out there that’s more so on the robbing drug dealers thing, or robbing criminals in general. Not the cheesy ones working with some “king pin” lol.

Would prefer realistic, or at least realistic-ish.


Series recommendations welcome too, unless that’s a no no.

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Copland/ the departed
2. To Live And Die In L.A.
3. 'Bad Lieutenant' (1992)

'Bad Lieutenant: Port of Call New Oreleans' (2009)
  - Harvey Keitel’s performance in the original is fucking incredible
4. Shaft (2000)

Serpico (1973)

Prince of the City (1981)

The Big Easy (1986)
5. American Gangster (2007)
6. The best crooked cop series ran from 2002 - 2008. It’s the shield. It was an FX show that pushed the boundaries. First time I heard “shit,” on regular cable. 


https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/The_Shield
  - The Shield is the GOAT when it comes to dirty cop stories.
7. Internal affairs
8. Dragged Across Concrete 

## 5. Need a good 80s movie!!

**Score:** 24
**Body:** Looking for one that has vibes like dirty dancing, breakfast club, sixteen candles, goonies, stand by me etc. Just some good old 80s vibes!

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Can’t Buy Me Love and Just One of the Guys. Trust me!
  - Just One of the Guys.

1:27:55
2. They Live!

Repo Man

The Fly

Short Circuit
3. Better off Dead.
4. 3 O’Clock High could be a fun one for you
5. •Some Kind Of Wonderful

•The Lost Boys
6. Ferris Bueller’s day off
  - That is so choice.
7. Fast Time at Ridgemont High, Trading Place, Police Academy
8. Say Anything.  You’re welcome.

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Mars Attacks(1996) This movie is fucking hilarious, and a memorable time back then.

**Score:** 2236

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Along with Independence Day, this was a very goddamn enjoyable sci-fi flick in theaters back in the day. The cast is solid, and Nicholson goes over the top.
  - Solid doesn’t quite do it justice. The ensemble was remarkably well put together.

Jack Nicholson,
Pierce Brosnan,
Glenn Close,
Annette Benning,
Danny DeVito,
Martin Short,
Michael J Fox,
Natalie Portman,
Pam Grier,
Jack Black,
    - Tom Jones!?!


Sarah Jessica Parker, Christina Applegate, Joe Don Baker, Ray J, Jim Brown, Lukas Haas, and Tim Burton's former girlfriend Lisa Marie.
  - Which Nicholson? The President or the hotel owner?
2. ACK
  - ACK ACK ACK!
    - Don’t run, we are your friends.
3. I was scared when I first saw this movie lmao. Was way way to young to be seeing Aliens using ray guns to poof people out of existence. 

Love it now though
4. One of my favorite movies from my childhood.
5. One of those movies that just feels like I rented it from blockbuster every time I watch it

## 2. Which movie did this for you?

**Score:** 1451

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Blade Runner (1982). And it was raining.
  - Hopefully one day
2. The Matrix.  I had absolutely no clue what I was getting into other than Keanu Reeves and Lawrence Fishburn were in it.  I didnt shut the fuck up about that film for 2 years.
3. In 92 or 93, I was really young and wanted to see a western for some reason, and my grandmother took me to see Tombstone at a matinee. We had a good time and she tends to fight the screen during a fight lol. RIP grandma. But I remember how strange it was, we went in the day but it was dark and cloudy with rain afterward.

I'm pretty sure i saw Gattaca and White Squall during the day. It wasn't raining or dark when I exited the theater but I remember that feeling of deep thought under dim lighting only for my eyes to have to adjust to the sunlight when I went outside.
4. Manchester by the sea
5. Schindler‘s List. I wasn‘t prepared for this movie.
6. The SpongeBob Movie
7. Interstellar in IMAX
8. Dark City
9. The Matrix.

## 3. Nuremberg was a terrible film, change my mind

**Score:** 562
**Body:** Besides Crowe being way too obese for the role, he and Shannon seem like the only ones that are acting in the film. Remi is way too melodramatic.

The movie is a massive over simplification of the trial, and basically a year 7 history lesson on WW2.

the way in which it ends is so stupid, the climatic court scene, rami's characters role, rami's characters development, the extremely telegraphed subtext, the closing scene.

It was just poorly written and directed and It just make for a really bad film

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Thought that was John Goodman
  - 1 glasses away from saying "stfu donnie! "
  - Goodman is way slimmer than that these days. Unrecognizable really.
2. Would highly recommend you watch [Judgement at Nuremberg](https://www.bbc.co.uk/iplayer/episode/m001qp0w) on iPlayer. Interested to know your thoughts.
  - Nuremberg 2025 should come with a disclaimer stating that Judgement at Nuremberg exists
    - This is actually the third depiction of the Nuremberg Trials.

The original was incredible, and will bring you to tears.

The second one included an extended completely uncensored video of the liberation of several Concentration Camps. It uh…it will unquestionably be the most difficult thing you’ll ever watch.

I haven’t seen the most recent one, but OP saying Russell Crowe was too overweight to play a famously obese piece of shit human…you had to giggle.
3. It doesn't seem as though you can share photos here but Goering was wildly obese when he was captured in 1945.  He was 260 and 5'6.  I'm 220 and 5'7.  He lost weight in jail but I don't see why forcing actors to go through these wild weight shifts is necessary, especially when portraying a guy like Goering.  

I'll agree the trail was a gross oversimplification, and that they could have sacrificed some of the subplots to add time to the trial.
  - I don't know where you got your numbers.

Goering was 5'9".  At capture he was 300+ lbs.  At his peak weight in 1935, some historians put him at about 400lbs in around 1935 (he was a maximum unit in some photos).  I believe it too, because at his fat peak, he looks about as wide as he does tall.

He was so big, the allies couldn't transport him in the standard 2 man fighter plane that they intended to use.  They had to wait for a cargo plane.

However, the allies got him off of morphine and put him on a diet plan with his prison meals,  I think he got down to about 220lbs by the trial.  It was honestly the best physical shape he has been in since his 20s.  His mind was sharper than ever too.  Goering had a genius level IQ and was one of the few Nazi leaders to speak fluent conversational English.  HOWEVER, Goering was pretty much always high on morphine and it made him lethargic, fat, and his wits sluggish.
4. Holy shit it's the guy from Mr robot
5. It was decent I thought.

## 4. What movie plot point went mostly unexplored but would be a banger on its own?

**Score:** 225
**Body:** The Choctaw Vampire Hunters in Sinners would be an amazing movie on its own. Hell, I’d be happy to see it for prestige tv.

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. The history of Pumpkin and Honey Bunny from Pulp Fiction
  - I’m sure it involved a lot of meth.
2. The opening sequence of Wolverine, where they show Wolverine and Sabertooth as unkillable mutant soldiers fighting in wars throughout history was a terrific idea for a movie. Sadly, they relegated the good part of the film to a brief preamble to a tedious mess.
3. Obviously the future wars in Terminator. I can’t believe they never made a sequel to T2.
4. What if John c Reilly and Will Ferrel got the job with Seth Rogan in Step Brothers. That could be a banger film
5. The movie Civil War (2024) underutilized its world-buiding capacity. Not sure if everyone knows, but they had a whole imaginary scenario behind the war, with at least 3 different parties dividing the USA. We did not get much information about any of it, it was more of a background thing, and instead they focused on the work of the press.


Maybe that is ok for a movie, but I feel they could have created an entire franchise out of this, or even a TV series. What originated the conflict? What's happening in Canada and Mexico at the time? Etc.
  - I think part of the point of it was that the conflict was convoluted and had been going on long enough for the factions to have become muddled and extremely splintered
  - Also, a whole movie about how Jesse Plemons' character came into possession of those sunglasses.
6. The augmented reality AI-chimpanzee from Lawnmower Man.
7. I recently saw “Scanners (1981)” for the first time.  Boy does it have a lot of potential for expanding its sub plots.  Corporations experimenting with drugs that give people Psionic Powers and exploiting those people .  An underground network of Psionics fighting back against those corporations and the government.  This movie could use an update and expansion.

## 5. Just saw Marty Supreme and I’m exhausted

**Score:** 59
**Body:** Not surprised, as we see which Safdie brother drives the angst model now.

And I mean it 100% in a good way, as i absolutely loved “Good Time” and “Uncut Gems.”

I’m being forced to reevaluate whether OBAA is my top film of the year now, and I will say I think Chalamet takes home his first Oscar ( sorry Leo). 

The confidence and charisma put forth is one for the ages, and the energy of this film is what one comes to expect from Safdie. 

Interesting to see what others think 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Timothy was brilliant and it was a well-made movie but boy was I glad to see the credits roll.

It did not pass my check the time test. I found myself checking the time a few times.
  - Oh I have this test too!
2. I was exhausted too, and not in a good way.  It was like watching The Bear.  Not for me.  Didnt care for any characters, and the plot.
  - So glad to hear that others feel that way about The Bear. I tried watching it multiple times and just couldn't get past the dialogue and constant yelling. 
3. I thought the female characters were so poorly inserted and underdeveloped it was laughable, and Marty was a POS. Many times I wondered why I was still watching. I think it was to make sure the dog was ok.
  - I mean isn't that the point? He was using everyone for his own benefit?
4. Great movie with incredible performances across the board. 

My biggest nitpick was the ending, but it was a wild ride either way.
5. Timmy Chameleon is a great actor with more top level performances under his belt than many stars in their late 50‘s.

And he is booked by the best, which also helps him to shine.
  - People love to drag him, but the talent is genuinely undeniable, he's a *great* actor
    - He was incredibly good as Henry V.

---

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. I watched over 100 movies in the past half a year. Here's my TOP-10.

**Score:** 50
**Body:** 1. Harakiri - 1962, Japan, director Masaki Kobayashi

It's amazing how a film about an old samurai telling his family story can be much more entertaining than any blockbuster film.

2. Come and See - 1985, USSR, Elem Klimov

The most visceral portrayal of war I have ever seen with genuine horror by child actors who were scared senseless on the set of the film just because Klimov wanted the most realistic depiction of WW2.

3. Apocalypse Now - 1979, USA, Francis Ford Coppola

An almost surreal movie about war and cult with fantastic soundtrack and Marlo Brando's colonel Kurtz is my favorite movie antagonist ever.

4. Wolfwalkers - 2020, Ireland, Tommy Moore, Ross Sterwart

A beautiful cartoon with unique style and great environmental, anti-colonial and feminist messages (similar to Princess Mononoke).

5. The Great Dictator - 1940, USA, Charles Chaplin

The final speech alone is a masterpiece, movie's anti-fascist pro-humanity message is timeless.

6. The Good, the Bad, and the Ugly - 1966, Italy, Sergio Leone

Great cinematography and iconic soundtrack.

7. Schindler's List - 1993, USA, Stephen Spielberg

The final scene with Oskar Schindler feeling regret that he couldn't save more Jews from the Holocaust brought me to tears. The Auschwitz scene is scarier than any horror.

8. Sherlock Jr. - 1924, USA, Buster Keaton

Insanely innovative films which basically embodies what makes cinema such a great artform.

9. The Passion of Joan of Arc - 1928, France, Theodore Dreyer

Insanely beautiful cinematography and fantastic gut-wrenching performance by the main actress.

10. The Banshees of Inishereen - 2022, Ireland, Martin McDonagh

Amazing philosophical tragicomedy with fantastic performance by Colin Farrell. Probably the wittiest humor I have seen on screen.

### Top 4 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Great list. I've heard of most of these except Wolfwalkers will have to check that one out. 

Apocalypse Now is one of my favorite movies. The Redux in 4k is mind blowing amazing in how good it looks and sounds. A.N. was actually an early adopter of surround sound (though I think the surrounds were mono originally but they went back and remixed to full surround). Coppola has an intro in the beginning where he talks about them painstakingly restoring everything. Fortunately he isn't weird about modernizing like Lucas. 

I will say that I wish I could get the Redux version without the French plantation though. It really kills the vibe of the movie. It's nice to have seen once but next time I watch I'll just hit chapter next on it.
2. Congrats on treating yourself to some fantastic films - and such precise, pithy summaries of them too.

I recently rewatched Apocalypse Now, after nearly 20 years. It’s always interesting the new perspective you take after such a time.

What struck me this time was I just wasn’t impressed with the helicopter surfing scene. It just tips over into glorification rather than ridicule. 

But I realised that the real heart of darkness, the key insight of the film is *not* Colonel Kurtz’s “the horror”. It’s actually the speech given by General Corman when the mission is laid out: 

*“There’s a conflict in every human heart, between the rational and the irrational, between good and evil…Every man has got a breaking point. You and I have them…”*

It is a masterfully constructed and delivered scene, which brings home that it’s not all about Kurtz.
3. I also watched Harakiri for the first time this year and thought it was a masterpiece. 

Where I live (Uruguay, South America), Kurosawa and Ozu are revered and often revisited but Kobayashi has been barely mentioned and I'm wondering why. I looked into the programming archive of my local cineclub and Kwaidan and Rebelllion have been programmed and praised but there's no mention of Harakiri at all.

## 2. I set out to catch-up on many of the classics/international films I hadn't seen, here's how I did.

**Score:** 25
**Body:** (Originally posted on r/iwatchedanoldmovie but removed so I'll try here)

So I've mostly kept up with newer releases or so for the bulk of the time that I've been more into movies, and have mostly only seen American films as far as the classics go (your *Godfather*s and *2001*s and such), and even in that category I had a ton of stuff I felt as though I should have seen that I never did, so I set out over the last couple months of the year to see if I could check a lot of these off of my list that had been there for several years in some cases.

I will give a quick plug for Kanopy, I'm not sponsored by them but if you have a library card they have a lot of classic/Criterion type films available to stream and I watched a lot of these on there. 

The list of things I watched, and then some highlights;

\* Suspiria

\* Oldboy

\* Raging Bull

\* Paris is Burning

\* Man with a Movie Camera

\* The Last Picture Show

\* Santa Sangre

\* Persona

\* The Seventh Seal

\* Eraserhead

\* Punch Drunk Love

\* Altered States

\* Bicycle Thieves

\* Mirror

\* After Hours

\* Mulholland Drive

\* Tetsuo: The Iron Man

\* Meshes of the Afternoon

\* The Machine That Kills Bad People

\* Ballet Mechanique

\* Inland Empire

\* Breathless

\* Aguirre, the Wrath of God

\* Rashomon

\* Daisies

\* Funeral Parade of Roses

\* An Optical Poem

\* Thimble Theater

\* The 400 Blows

\* Jeanne Dielman, 23, quai du Commerce, 1080 Bruxelles

And my favorites of these;

**Persona:** Just a fascinating film from frame one to the very end, amazing how contemporary this feels compared to something like *The Seventh Seal* from just ten years earlier.

Was very cool seeing how much some of these films influenced more recent things I've loved, and this was definitely one of the big inspirations for *The Lighthouse*. This also has to be one of my favorite black and white films on a visual level, what this film does with shadow is just mind-blowing.

**The Last Picture Show:** Perhaps the most melancholic film I've seen, the shot of the stop sign lazily hanging in the wind is one of my new favorite shots in anything ever, the grit and the haze just gives it such a texture that makes this town feel so lived in and real.

It was also one of the few films I've seen that takes a much less nostalgic approach to depicting the 1950s, instead showing it as a time of struggle, just like any other.

Also, both this and *Santa Sangre* have scenes where a group of characters hire a prostitute to sleep with a character that has special needs, which isn't a lot, but it's weird that it happened twice. This was definitely one of the earliest American films I've seen that managed to incorporate sexuality and nudity in something that was still tasteful and dramatic, which surprised me given the year of release.

**Oldboy:** The only film from the 2000s on this list, but what a facemelter of a movie this is. I knew some of what the twist was unfortunately just from osmosis, but the full extent of the reveal genuinely had my jaw on the floor. Nasty in the best way.

**Jeanne Dielman:** This one kind of became my white whale, after the first hour I declared I had seen enough and didn't feel as though I would watch the rest of it, but the curiosity kept gnawing at me, even though I knew exactly what I was in for. Maybe there's something to that, I definitely felt the trancelike quality it was going for at points, but in equal measure it did feel like an endurance test. Glad I saw it even if I feel as though it's hard to really critique a film that's this much of an intentional stylistic challenge without sounding like "my six year old could've painted that".

**Mulholland Drive:** I felt like this was the first Lynch project that really clicked for me on an emotional level, seeing it all tie together how it manages these themes of the relation between actor and character, blurring the lines between performance and reality, it all just made sense in a way that was incredibly satisfying having struggled with some of Lynch's other work. However, this did lead to me finally watching *Twin Peaks: The Return*, which I absolutely adored.

**Inland Empire:** This was the other one I was perhaps most curious about as the most daunting of Lynch films, but the combination of the creepy video look and the gargantuan runtime create such a unique sense of getting lost that few films manage to capture, let alone ones that tap into such a specific subconscious nerve. It's a mood more than anything, but I really dug this one as just something completely on the far end of abstraction, as opposed to some of his films which land more in the middle.

**Meshes of the Afternoon:** Continuing the Lynch trend, this was a very cool silent short film that I believe was one of his inspirations for *Eraserhead*, and you can see how once you watch it for yourself. It's also on YouTube for free just FYI.

**Man with a Movie Camera:** As someone who really enjoys going on trips, filming places and setting the footage to music, this was like the great great great granddaddy of what people are now doing with travel videos on the internet lol. Surprisingly engaging and entertaining, fascinating how much wouldn't exist without this. The desire to document the common things around us has existed for so long, and it was really cool to get to witness more regular day to day life in a historical period outside of the context of some major event.

Anyhoo, my hands hurt from typing now so I'll shut up but hopefully some of y'all enjoy any of this or have seen some of these as well. Any recommendations would be welcome also, and appreciate if you took the time to read any of this. I really value this sub as one of the few places on reddit where I feel like I can actually have good faith discussions without things descending into slap fights lol. 

peace, and happy new year yall  


### Top 0 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)


## 3. Question on A Woman under the Influence film

**Score:** 8
**Body:** What is going on between Mabel and her parents? specifically the two scenes that come to mind are when she’s burying herself on her dad on the couch, and the way in which her mother interacts with her at the beginning of the film when taking the kids…they both seem really checked out and disconnected from her until that moment when the mom understands Mabel’s statement about wanting her dad to stand up for her…I’m just curious if anyone can extrapolate something more about her relationship with them and how it connects to her deteriorating mental health

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Mabels parents may not be equipped with the right tools to help Mabel and are doing their best. They may be burnt out, in denial, or hoping for the best,  as many did in those times. 

Both of Mabels parents weren’t actors as far as I remember so perhaps some non-actor weirdness is coming through also. 
2. Gena Rowland’s real mother played her mother in the film, just like John Cassavete’s mother played his. I think her interactions with her parents show us that she has never been understood by anyone and that her quirks have always kept her from being totally accepted. She has a loneliness and self doubt because nobody who is supposed to love her can see and accept her point of view. The scene with her begging him to stand up for her, and him physically standing up with that dumb smile on his face like he’s indulging the whims of a child, is just the most heartbreaking thing

## 4. Realized something watching Inception, Nolan is lifting a key plot move from Homer’s Iliad and has been thinking about Homer since Inception or before...

**Score:** 8
**Body:** I was rewatching Inception and something clicked that I can’t unsee now.

In the Iliad, Zeus deliberately tricks Agamemnon by sending him a false dream. The dream implants the idea that now is the moment to attack Troy and Agamemnon acts on it, taking it almost as if it were his own original idea. The result is war and disaster, exactly as Zeus intended. My point is that Zeus doesn’t force Agamemnon. *He plants the idea through a dream.*

That’s basically the core mechanism of Inception.

Cobb doesn’t control Fischer directly but instead he implants an idea deep enough that Fischer believes it’s his own, leading him to dismantle his father’s company. Different stakes, different tech, but its the same structural move...dreams used as weapons for power and control.

What’s interesting is that Christopher Nolan went to the Odyssey first instead of making the Iliad, but this dream deception plot is straight out of the Iliad, which actually comes earlier. Maybe the Iliad was too big, too violent, too politically grim to tackle directly, but the idea has clearly survived and he was obviously thinking about Homer for more than a decade now.

Curious what others think?

### Top 1 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. https://variety.com/2025/film/news/christopher-nolan-hired-troy-director-the-odyssey-1236588697/

> Christopher Nolan confirmed to Empire Magazine (via World of Reel) that Warner Bros. originally hired him to direct its swords-and-sandals epic “Troy,” more than two decades before Nolan would mount his adaptation of Homer’s “The Odyssey.” The events depicted in the former movie factor into the latter, which is why the Oscar winner told the publication mounting his film version of “The Odyssey” did not come out of nowhere despite some fans being surprised Nolan would want to tackle Homer’s Greek epic.

There ya go.

Despite this I reckon you need more evidence to confirm the plot points of INCEPTION are "straight out" of THE ILIAD. INCEPTION was originally drafted about lucid dreaming, and lucid dreams use various practices to prompt and seed dream experiences. 

As for Nolan being familiar with Homer, you'd hope so.
  - I didn’t say plot points, I said the core mechanism of the plot is planting a dream into someone’s mind to get them to act or do something they had not really been sure about is straight out of Book 2 of the Iliad. And Now Nolan is doing the Odyssey and you’re confirming he was hired long ago to do a Troy style movie which I didn’t know. 

So at this point my interpretation is more than just evidence. It seems overwhelmingly true.

## 5. Could a fictional movie in the style of James Joyce's Ulysses work?

**Score:** 9
**Body:** Currently in the middle of James Joyce's Ulysses.  An incredibly difficult book to say the least, one I find alternately agonizing and fascinating.   

What I find most captivating is the format of the book: Joyce incorporates a multitude of writing styles; in each chapter, a different style can be found.  The book runs the gamut from stream-of-consciousness to 19th century sentimental women's novels to editorials to play format and more.  In one chapter alone, "Oxen of the Sun," the style shifts on a dime, with Joyce parodying over 30 different styles of prose by canonized authors such as John Milton, Sir Thomas Malory, Thomas Huxley, John Bunyan, Charles Dickens, and more. 

We've seen movies mix genres and even mix a few styles very effectively.  We've also seen directors mimic the style of auteurs.  But in Ulysses, Joyce parodies and changes the writing style depending on the scene and the narrator, and the style can shift suddenly without warning.  What do you make of a movie if a director brings that same thought process to a movie? In other words, a movie that constantly shifts to a specific style of director depending on the scene?  

For example, in one scene, perhaps during a conversation between the main character and his family members in a household where marriage is discussed, the style of the film could replicate that of an Ozu film, replete with tatami-shots.  Then during an action scene, while the main character is engaged in a shoot-out with adversaries, the movie's style replicates a John Woo film, with a lot of bloodshed and tons of edits.  When the main character has to choose between two women, it becomes an Eric Rohmer film with garrulous characters.  During a flashback, the movie shifts to become a black and white silent film emulating a work by Griffith/Murnau/Lang etc.  In another scene of the movie, when the protagonist is reflective, it shifts to a film reminiscent of a Tarkovsky or Bergman film.  When he has a dream, it becomes Bunuelian/Lynchian.  The movie could even parody Hallmark movies complete with a sappy sentimental ending peppered with trite and cliched dialogue.

I don't know any one single director who could pull this off.  But I think it would be fascinating experiment for one to try. 

What do you think?  Could it work?  Would you watch it?

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It doesn't try at all to translate the novel's style onscreen, but the 1967 film adaptation is actually a pretty good and definitely underrated film. The Dead is clearly the best Joycean film, but Ulysses (1967) is worth a watch.

As for a cinematic equivalent, have you ever seen Millennium Actress? It's an animated love letter to Japanese cinema with homages to various styles, from Kurosawa samurai movie to Ozu to Godzilla.
2. Oliver Stone's *Natural Born Killers* kind of does this. It even takes the style changes one step further and utilizes 35mm, 16mm, Super 8mm, Hi8 video, betacam video, and traditional cell animation to signify shifts.
3. I think it could work if there was an actual story or reason behind it otherwise it would feel gimmicky (potentially to an obnoxious degree) 

Ulysses manages to exercise all these styles but there’s still an actual story with emotion that runs throughout, and the form is there to serve a purpose and evoke a feeling rather than simply being there for the sake of it (essentially showing off) 

Ultimately boils down to any the same issue with any other story, is there a strong motive or reason that will make anyone actually care about it?

Edit: also it would probably be incredibly expensive, a novel can switch styles at the writers will but a film would have to arrange for numerous different sets, potentially different cameras, different lighting rigs etc for each different style
  - Have you seen Millennium Actress? If so, do you think it's a good example of using stylistic homages in a narrative context?
4. Steven Soderbergh's "Schizopolis" (1996), Olver Stone's "Natural Born Killers" (1994) and Francis Ford Coppola's student film "You're a Big Boy Now" (1966) all come to mind as films that suddenly and unexpectedly shift narrative dialects.
5. I love this idea, and as a major Joyce fan have often wanted movies that try something like this.

* There’s a bunch of directors who have very credibly mimicked several different styles, like Soderbergh, Tarantino, Van Sant, maybe Woody Allen. But their approach appears to be to be a few key reference points for each project+a few twists, so you don’t end up with this intense journey through styles, modes, and references. Someone like Soderbergh probably could do it, but I don’t think he’s interested in that.
* There’s separately a few filmmakers that really go for a more-is-more wild journey through rapidly shifting tones and visual styles like Fellini and Luhrmann. There’re elements of borrowing and parody in their works, but in the end the “borrowing”/syncretism takes a back seat to their own signature styles.

So with that in mind, the closest I’ve seen are:

* Everything Everywhere All at Once
* the Spiderverse movies
* 32 Short Films About Glenn Gould (Girard), though it’s highly structured
* a great Joyce adaptation: Passages from Finnegan’s Wake (Mary Ellen Bute), much more stylistically eclectic than the Strick Ulysses.
* Pierrot Le Fou (Godard) sort of, and arguably Film Socialisme 
* Forbidden Room (Maddin) and to some extent a few of his others, like Cowards Bend the Knee.
* Holy Motors, sort of
* Natural Born Killers
* Fast Cheap and Out of Control (Morris, dp. Bob Richardson, same as NBK)
* More loosely, Welles’s F is for Fake and some of the essay films by Chris Marker.

There’s enough success here that I think this type of movie is possible and could easily be amazing. Would love to hear other suggestions!
6. I don’t see the point to be honest. Under the Volcano is a similarly unfilmable modernist novel that they made a decent stab at I guess, but Ulysses is far more difficult than even that.
7. My gut reaction is that stylist changes work better in books than in films because the latter is usually consumed in one sitting while novels (and especially those as long as Ulysses) take much longer to get through. You read a few chapters then sleep or think about it, and then resume again the next day.
8. I suppose it would be possible to do a film with a basic story but the aesthetics/presentation evolve as the story progresses. Easiest would be just starting it off like silent films and following the evolution of styles. Throw in some obvious homages to specific films and that’s as close as you can likely get.
  - Great idea, and that reminds me of how Joyce's first novel, A Portrait of an Artist as Young Man, was written.  It starts off with child-like language and becomes more sophisticated as the main characters grows and matures.  Similarly, I could see a movie starting off in black and white, silent, shot crudely and clumsily even, with the style becoming more and more refined and polished as the movie traces the evolution of the main character - as the protagonist progresses, so does the camera movement and style.  Not sure if a movie has been told this way yet, but I would be interested in seeing it.
